teachingHint: Your child is learning that 'aw' makes the /or/ sound (as in 'paw'). This is a new way to spell a sound they already know from Phase 3. Help them spot 'aw' and blend: p-aw → paw, d-r-aw → draw. The underlined words are for your child to read — you can help with the rest!

phonicsWords.teachingTip: p-aw → 'aw' makes the /or/ sound. Blend: p-aw → paw
s-aw → 'aw' makes the /or/ sound. Blend: s-aw → saw
c-l-aw → 'aw' makes the /or/ sound. Blend: cl-aw → claw
r-aw → 'aw' makes the /or/ sound. Blend: r-aw → raw

bonusWord.teachingTip: Tricky word! The 'ed' sounds like /d/. We say 'calld'. Learn it by sight.

story: The cat called out and held up a paw. It saw a claw mark on the raw fish!

teachingHint: Your child is learning that 'aw' makes the /or/ sound (as in 'paw'). This is a new way to spell a sound they already know from Phase 3. Help them spot 'aw' and blend: p-aw → paw, d-r-aw → draw. The underlined words are for your child to read — you can help with the rest!

l-aw → 'aw' makes the /or/ sound. Blend: l-aw → law
d-r-aw → 'aw' makes the /or/ sound. Blend: dr-aw → draw
y-aw-n → 'aw' makes the /or/ sound. Blend: y-aw-n → yawn

bonusWord.teachingTip: Tricky word! The 'ed' sounds like /t/. We say 'lookt'. Learn it by sight.

story: He looked at the jaw of the big shark. Can you draw it with a yawn? It is the law of the sea!

teachingHint: Your child is learning that 'aw' makes the /or/ sound (as in 'paw'). This is a new way to spell a sound they already know from Phase 3. Help them spot 'aw' and blend: p-aw → paw, d-r-aw → draw. The underlined words are for your child to read — you can help with the rest!

phonicsWords.teachingTip: d-aw-n → 'aw' makes the /or/ sound. Blend: d-aw-n → dawn
f-aw-n → 'aw' makes the /or/ sound. Blend: f-aw-n → fawn
l-aw-n → 'aw' makes the /or/ sound. Blend: l-aw-n → lawn
s-t-r-aw → 'aw' makes the /or/ sound. Blend: str-aw → straw

bonusWord.teachingTip: Tricky word! 'Mr' is short for 'Mister'. We say 'mister'. Learn it by sight.

story: At dawn, Mr Fox saw a fawn on the lawn. It was drinking from a cup with a straw!

teachingHint: Your child is learning that 'aw' makes the /or/ sound (as in 'paw'). This is a new way to spell a sound they already know from Phase 3. Help them spot 'aw' and blend: p-aw → paw, d-r-aw → draw. The underlined words are for your child to read — you can help with the rest!

phonicsWords.teachingTip: c-r-aw-l → 'aw' makes the /or/ sound. Blend: cr-aw-l → crawl
sh-aw-l → 'aw' makes the /or/ sound. Blend: sh-aw-l → shawl
p-aw → 'aw' makes the /or/ sound. Blend: p-aw → paw
c-l-aw → 'aw' makes the /or/ sound. Blend: cl-aw → claw

bonusWord.teachingTip: Tricky word! 'Mrs' is short for 'Missus'. We say 'missiz'. Learn it by sight.

story: Mrs Bear put on her shawl and began to crawl. She held up a paw and showed her claw!

teachingHint: Your child is learning that 'au' makes the /or/ sound (as in 'launch'). This is another new way to spell the /or/ sound. Help them spot 'au' and blend: h-au-l → haul, l-au-n-ch → launch. The underlined words are for your child to read — you can help with the rest!

phonicsWords.teachingTip: h-au-l → 'au' makes the /or/ sound. Blend: h-au-l → haul
l-au-n-ch → 'au' makes the /or/ sound. Blend: l-au-n-ch → launch
P-au-l → 'au' makes the /or/ sound. Blend: P-au-l → Paul
c-au-s-e → 'au' makes the /or/ sound. Blend: c-au-se → cause

bonusWord.teachingTip: Tricky word! The 'ed' sounds like /t/. We say 'arskt'. Learn it by sight.

story: Paul asked if he could launch the boat. He had to haul it to the water. What was the cause of the delay?

teachingHint: Your child is learning that 'au' makes the /or/ sound (as in 'launch'). This is another new way to spell the /or/ sound. Help them spot 'au' and blend: h-au-l → haul, l-au-n-ch → launch. The underlined words are for your child to read — you can help with the rest!

phonicsWords.teachingTip: p-au-s-e → 'au' makes the /or/ sound. Blend: p-au-se → pause
s-au-c-e → 'au' makes the /or/ sound. Blend: s-au-ce → sauce
f-au-l-t → 'au' makes the /or/ sound. Blend: f-au-lt → fault
v-au-l-t → 'au' makes the /or/ sound. Blend: v-au-lt → vault

bonusWord.teachingTip: Tricky word! 'People' doesn't follow normal rules. We say 'pee-pul'. Learn it by sight.

story: Some people like sauce on their chips. Let's pause — it's not my fault the vault is shut!

teachingHint: Your child is learning that 'au' makes the /or/ sound (as in 'launch'). This is another new way to spell the /or/ sound. Help them spot 'au' and blend: h-au-l → haul, l-au-n-ch → launch. The underlined words are for your child to read — you can help with the rest!

l-au-n-ch → 'au' makes the /or/ sound. Blend: l-au-n-ch → launch

bonusWord.teachingTip: Tricky word! The 'oul' sounds like /oo/. We say 'cud'. Learn it by sight.

story: Paul could not haul the big box. Inside was sauce and a rocket to launch into space!

teachingHint: Your child already knows the /oa/ sound from Phase 3 (as in 'boat'). Now they're learning that 'oe' makes the same sound, usually at the end of a word: t-oe → toe, f-oe → foe. The underlined words are for your child to read — you can help with the rest!

phonicsWords.teachingTip: t-oe → 'oe' makes the /oa/ sound. Blend: t-oe → toe
f-oe → 'oe' makes the /oa/ sound. Blend: f-oe → foe
d-oe → 'oe' makes the /oa/ sound. Blend: d-oe → doe
h-oe → 'oe' makes the /oa/ sound. Blend: h-oe → hoe

bonusWord.teachingTip: Tricky word! 'Oh' doesn't follow normal rules. The 'h' is silent. We say 'oh'. Learn it by sight.

story: Oh no! I stubbed my toe! A doe ran past and a foe grabbed the hoe from the shed.

teachingHint: Your child already knows the /oa/ sound from Phase 3 (as in 'boat'). Now they're learning that 'oe' makes the same sound, usually at the end of a word: t-oe → toe, f-oe → foe. The underlined words are for your child to read — you can help with the rest!

phonicsWords.teachingTip: J-oe → 'oe' makes the /oa/ sound. Blend: J-oe → Joe
w-oe → 'oe' makes the /oa/ sound. Blend: w-oe → woe
t-oe → 'oe' makes the /oa/ sound. Blend: t-oe → toe
f-oe → 'oe' makes the /oa/ sound. Blend: f-oe → foe

bonusWord.teachingTip: Tricky word! 'Their' doesn't follow normal rules. We say 'thair'. Learn it by sight.

story: Joe felt such woe! Their toe got stuck. Was a foe to blame? No, it was just a bump!
